Summarize in up to four sentences.: Stocks wobble with Fed minutes on tap: Stock market news today
Brett LoGiurato and Josh Schafer
Wed, August 16, 2023 at 12:36 p.m. GMT-4·1 min read
In this article:

TGT
+3.52%


^DJI
-0.02%


^GSPC
-0.15%


^IXIC
-0.47%

US stocks wobbled in midday trading Wednesday as a retail-heavy week continued with a warning from Target (TGT) and Wall Street awaited the release of minutes from the Federal Reserve's latest policy meeting.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average (^DJI) was roughly flat, while the S&P 500 (^GSPC) was down 0.2%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (^IXIC) was down 0.7%. The indexes were looking to rebound from a day that saw each lose more than 1%.

Target took center stage Wednesday morning as the retailer slashed its full-year profit outlook and issued a warning about general economic malaise. The company cited rising interest rates and uncertainty from the restart of student loan repayments as factors that may continue to affect its bottom line. However, the stock jumped more than 3% at the open as it revealed a profit jump.

In the afternoon, the Fed will be back in focus, as the minutes from last month's policy meeting are set for release at 2 p.m. ET. The central bank raised interest rates to their highest point since 2001 at that meeting. Investors will look for any clues to what the Fed might do at its meeting next month. At this point, according to the CME Group's FedWatch tool, over 90% of traders expect the bank to hold steady.

LIVE UPDATES

12 hours ago
Josh Schafer
Stocks lose steam in afternoon trading
After starting the day mixed, all three of the major averages are now in the red as investors digest a warning on the US consumer from Target and await minutes from the Federal Reserves July policy meeting which are scheduled for release at 2 p.m.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average (^DJI) fell just along the flat line, while the S&P 500 (^GSPC) slipped 0.2%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (^IXIC) led the losses falling 0.7%.

13 hours ago
Josh Schafer
Target shares higher despite gloomy outlook
Target (TGT) stock rose nearly 3% on Wednesday as the company reported better than expected earnings per share for the prior quarter and showed improvements in gross margins and inventories. But it wasn't all good for the retailer.

Yahoo Finance's Brian Sozzi reports:

In its second quarter earnings report, the discounter slashed its full-year profit outlook on Tuesday, warning that general economic malaise, rising interest rates, and uncertainty from the restart of student loan repayments in a few weeks may continue to take their collective toll.

"The resumption of student loan repayments is one of many factors that we're watching really closely," Target CFO Michael Fiddelke told Yahoo Finance on a media call.

Target CEO Brian Cornell added that the back-to-school season has started on a "solid" note but warned that much of the key shopping period lay ahead of the retailer.

Yet another cautious outlook from Target arrives as the big-box retailer continues to battle a more price-competitive Walmart (WMT) and the fallout from consumer uproar over Pride Month merchandise.

"Certainly, during the month of June we did see some behavior from guests that caused our teams to feel unsafe at work," Cornell said on the Pride Month fallout. "And we certainly saw some angry guests that were intimidating our team members and damaging merchandise and defacing some of the signage. Once we took those actions and addressed the situation, we certainly saw things normalize and we certainly think we took the right steps during that moment in time."

14 hours ago
Dani Romero
Builders boost new construction as inventory slump persists
Homebuilders aren’t slowing down amid persistent inventory woes. But they also aren’t as bullish on future developments.

Housing starts for both new single- and multi-family units increased to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.452 million units, according to the Census Bureau data out Wednesday. That’s 3.9% higher than June’s revised estimate rate of 1.398 million units and 5.9% higher from a year ago. That pace just exceeded the 1.450 million units economists surveyed by Bloomberg projected.

Permits to build came in at an annualized rate of 1.442 million units in July, just a 0.1% gain from the revised June rate of 1.441 million units and lower than economists' expectations of 1.463 million units. Single-family dwellings permits rose 0.6% from the month prior to 924,000 units. On the multifamily side, the pace delivered a rate of 464,000 units in July.

For much of the year, homebuilders have been optimistic as a limited supply of previously-owned homes sparked more interest in new construction. But with mortgage rates now hovering near 7%, consumers are again feeling the pinch of affordability, and builders have noticed. The latest builder sentiment dipped for the first time in August, after seven consecutive months of increases.

"Rising mortgage rates and high construction costs stemming from a dearth of construction workers, a lack of buildable lots and ongoing shortages of distribution transformers put a chill on builder sentiment in August," Alicia Huey, NAHB chairman and a homebuilder from Birmingham, Ala., said in a statement on Tuesday.

Full story

15 hours ago
Josh Schafer
Trending stocks in morning trade
Target (TGT) led the Yahoo Finance trending tickers page on Wednesday morning. Shares of the retailer gained more than 6% after the company's quarterly results came in largely better than feared, including improved gross margins and shrinking inventories. However, the company slashed it's full-year profit outlook.

Shares of VinFast Auto (VFS) fell more than 20% after shares of the EV automaker surged following its Nasdaq trading debut.

Cava (CAVA) stock was up more than 1% after the company reported its first profitable quarter. Revenue grew 62.4% compared to the prior quarter, jumping to $171.1 million from $105.3 million previously.

Coinbase (COIN) stock popped more than 2% after the crypto currency exchange said it received regulatory approval to offer US retail customers regulated crypto futures in the coming months.

15 hours ago
Josh Schafer
Stocks open lower
Stocks were slightly in the red at the open on Wednesday as Target (TGT) warned of a cautious consumer in the back half of the year and investors awaited the release of minutes from the Federal Reserve's latest policy meeting.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average (^DJI) hovered along the flat line, as the S&P 500 (^GSPC) slipped 0.1%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (^IXIC) led losses falling 0.4%.

16 hours ago
Jenny Mccall
Target, Coinbase, Tesla: Stocks trending in premarket trading
Here are some of the stocks leading Yahoo Finance’s trending tickers page in premarket trading on Wednesday:

Target (TGT): The retailer's shares rose 7% pre market on Wednesday as a profit gain appeared to outweigh a slash to its full-year profit outlook.

Coinbase (COIN): Shares rose by 2% after the group announced it had secured approval to offer cryptocurrency futures to US retail customers.

JD.com (JD): Shares in the Chinese tech group fell 5% after it beat Wall Street estimates for second-quarter revenue on Wednesday. Investors, however, are still concerned by China’s poor economic outlook.

Tesla (TSLA): Shares in the EV maker fell 2% premarket as it continued to slash prices of its cars.

17 hours ago
Myles Udland
Futures little-changed as markets look to rebound
After a shaky session for US stocks that saw all three major averages drop more than 1% futures were fractionally higher early Wednesday with investors digesting results from Target (TGT) ahead of Thursday's marquee quarterly report of the week from Walmart (WMT). Target stock was up as much as 7.4% in pre-market trade on Wednesday.

Near 8:05 a.m. ET, futures tied to the Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq were all higher by less than 0.1%.

Elsewhere in markets, crude oil prices were up about 0.3% with WTI crude oil trading near $81.30 a barrel while gold was little changed near $1,934 an ounce, a nearly three-year high.

US stocks experienced volatility on Wednesday as the market awaited the release of the Federal Reserve's meeting minutes and reacted to a warning from Target regarding economic uncertainty. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was relatively flat, while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite both saw declines. Target's shares initially jumped over 3% despite the retailer's reduced profit outlook due to factors such as rising interest rates and the restart of student loan repayments. Additionally, homebuilders have maintained steady construction despite inventory shortages, but are becoming more cautious due to rising mortgage rates and construction costs.
"Becoming more cautious", how?

Homebuilders are becoming more cautious due to several factors. Rising mortgage rates are making housing less affordable for consumers, which could potentially dampen demand for new homes. Additionally, high construction costs resulting from a shortage of construction workers and buildable lots, as well as ongoing shortages of distribution transformers, are also impacting builder sentiment. This caution is reflected in the latest dip in builder sentiment, marking the first decline in seven consecutive months of increases.
What are distribution transformers?

Distribution transformers are electrical devices used to transfer electrical energy from a primary distribution circuit to a secondary distribution circuit. They are typically used in the power grid to step down voltage levels, allowing electricity to be safely distributed to homes, businesses, and other end-users. These transformers play a crucial role in the distribution of electrical power and ensuring that voltage levels are appropriate for consumption. Shortages of distribution transformers can impact the construction and expansion of power distribution infrastructure, thus affecting the operations of homebuilders and the overall housing market.
Is that the same thing as a transformer?

Yes, a distribution transformer is a type of transformer. Transformers, in general, are electrical devices that are used to transfer electrical energy between different voltage levels or currents. They consist of primary and secondary coils, which are wound around an iron core. The primary coil receives electrical energy at one voltage level and the secondary coil delivers it at a different voltage level. Distribution transformers specifically focus on stepping down high voltage levels, typically used in transmission systems, to lower voltage levels suitable for distribution and consumption.
Name an example of a transformer that is not a distribution transformer.

An example of a transformer that is not a distribution transformer is a power transformer. Power transformers are used in electrical power systems to step up or step down voltage levels for transmission purposes. They are typically found in substations where electricity is being transferred from power plants to distribution networks. Power transformers are designed to handle higher voltage levels, typically in the range of thousands of volts, compared to the lower voltage levels that distribution transformers handle.
Name yet another example.

Another example of a transformer that is not a distribution transformer is an isolation transformer. Isolation transformers are used to provide electrical isolation between the input and output circuits. They are commonly found in electronic devices, such as audio equipment and medical devices, to prevent electrical noise or interference from affecting the connected equipment. Isolation transformers do not necessarily step up or step down voltage levels like power transformers or distribution transformers, but rather focus on providing electrical isolation and maintaining the same voltage levels on both input and output sides.
So, are isolation transformers essentially dynamically altering their step-ups and step-downs?

No, isolation transformers do not dynamically alter their step-ups and step-downs. In fact, isolation transformers do not generally change the voltage level at all. Their primary function is to provide electrical isolation, meaning they physically and electrically separate the input and output circuits. They typically have a 1:1 turns ratio, which means there is no voltage transformation or change in voltage level between the primary and secondary coils. The purpose of an isolation transformer is to ensure that there is no direct electrical connection between the input and output circuits, preventing electrical noise, interference, or ground loops from affecting the connected devices.
So, the connection is magnetic?

Yes, the connection in a transformer is magnetic. Transformers work based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. When an alternating current (AC) flows through the primary coil of a transformer, it creates a changing magnetic field. This magnetic field then induces a voltage in the secondary coil, which is connected to the load. The magnetic field allows for the transfer of electrical energy from the primary to the secondary coil through mutual induction. There is no physical or direct electrical connection between the primary and secondary coils; the energy transfer occurs through the magnetic field coupling between the coils. This magnetic coupling enables the transformation of voltage or current levels in the transformer.
What is a ground loop? Explain by using no more than four sentences.

A ground loop occurs when there are multiple paths to ground in an electrical system. It can cause unwanted interference and noise in audio or video signals. The loop forms when there are voltage differences between grounds in different devices or circuits, leading to circulating current. This circulating current can result in audible hum, distorted signals, or visual artifacts. Proper grounding techniques and isolating ground connections can help eliminate ground loops and mitigate their effects.
How does the isolation of the isolation transformer prevent ground loops? Explain by using no more than four sentences.

The isolation provided by an isolation transformer can help prevent ground loops by breaking the direct electrical connection between the input and output circuits. By physically and electrically separating the circuits, there is no direct path for circulating currents to flow and create ground loops. This eliminates the potential voltage differences between the grounds of different devices or circuits that contribute to the formation of ground loops. Isolation transformers provide a clean, isolated ground reference, minimizing the risk of ground loop interference.
Is the problem with ground loops because their voltage is not always exactly 0?

Yes, the problem with ground loops stems from the presence of voltage differences between different ground points in an electrical system. In an ideal scenario, all ground points would have a voltage of exactly 0, providing a common reference point for all connected devices. However, in practical situations, there can be small differences in voltage potential due to various factors, such as resistance, impedance, or current flow. These voltage differences can create circulating currents in the ground loop, leading to interference and noise in the system. The isolation provided by an isolation transformer helps eliminate the voltage differences and prevents the formation of ground loops, resulting in a cleaner and more stable electrical environment.
